learningObjectives: **Set up any probability problem** from scratch — define the sample space $\Omega$, identify the event $A$, and compute $\mathbb{P}(A)$ for uniform probability spaces.
**Apply complementary counting** to handle “at least one” problems, including the Birthday Paradox and its generalisation.
**Use the Inclusion-Exclusion Principle** to compute probabilities involving overlapping events and consecutive patterns.
**Count arrangements under constraints** — circular permutations, non-adjacent selections, and the block trick for consecutive groups.
**Derive and apply the derangement formula**, and know that $\mathbb{P}(\text{derangement}) \approx 1/e$ for $n \geq 5$.
**Solve geometric probability problems** by mapping events onto regions and computing areas — including chord intersection and semicircle containment.
**Recognise and exploit symmetry** as the fastest path to an answer in interview settings.

blocks.content: Probabilityisthemathematicsofuncertainty.Inquantitativefinanceandtrading,everymodel,everybet,andeveryriskestimaterestsonprobabilisticfoundations.Thegoalofthistopicistobuildthe*problem-solving toolkit*thatletsyoucrackinterviewquestionsunderpressurein5–10minutes.

blocks.title: The Three Ingredients of Any Probability Problem
blocks.content:
\begin{enumerate}\item **Sample space** $\Omega$: the set of all possible outcomes.

\item **Event** $A \subseteq \Omega$: outcomes you care about.

\item **Probability measure** $\mathbb{P}$: $\mathbb{P}(\Omega)=1$, $\mathbb{P}(\emptyset)=0$, countable additivity.\end{enumerate} For **uniform** spaces: $\mathbb{P}(A) = |A|/|\Omega| = \text{favourable}/\text{total}$.
**Interview strategy:**(1)Identify$\Omega$.(2)Count$|A|$—directlyorviacomplementarycounting/IEP.(3)Sanity-checkboundarycases.

blocks.title: Key Formulas
blocks.content: \begin{align*}\text{Permutations (ordered):}\quad&P(n,k) = \frac{n!}{(n-k)!}\\[4pt] \text{Combinations (unordered):}\quad&\dbinom{n}{k}= \frac{n!}{k!\,(n-k)!}\end{align*} *Order matters $\Rightarrow$ permutation. Order doesn't $\Rightarrow$ combination.*
**Majority Heads in Three Coin Tosses.** Flip a fair coin three times. What is the probability of getting more heads than tails?
blocks.solution:
$|\Omega|=8$. Favourable (\#H $\geq 2$): $\{$HHH, HHT, HTH, THH$\}$ = 4 outcomes. \[\mathbb{P} = \frac{4}{8}= \frac{1}{2}\] **Slick:** Odd tosses $\Rightarrow$ no ties $\Rightarrow$ by symmetry $\mathbb{P}(\#H>\#T)=\mathbb{P}(\#T>\#H)=\tfrac{1}{2}$.

**Key takeaway:** For any odd number of fair coin tosses, majority heads has probability $\tfrac{1}{2}$ by symmetry — no calculation needed.

blocks.content:
**Parity of Heads in Coin Tosses.** Flip 10 fair coins. What is the probability the number of heads is even?

*Hint: try the algebraic trick $(p+(1-p))^{n* \pm ((1-p)-p)^{n}$.}
blocks.solution:
**Method 1 — Direct:** $|A|=\dbinom{10}{0}+\dbinom{10}{2}+\cdots+\dbinom{10}{10}=512$, $\mathbb{P}=512/1024=\tfrac{1}{2}$.

**Method 2 — Algebraic trick:** $\mathbb{P}(\text{even heads}) = \dfrac{1+(1-2p)^n}{2}\Big|_{p=1/2}= \dfrac{1}{2}$ for all $n\geq1$.

**Method 3 — Recurrence:** $a_{n} = \tfrac{1}{2}$ satisfies $a_{n} = p(1-a_{n-1})+(1-p)a_{n-1}$ when $a_{n-1}=\tfrac{1}{2}$.

**Key takeaway:** Parity of heads is always $\tfrac{1}{2}$ for any $n\geq1$ fair coin tosses.

blocks.title: Complementary Counting
blocks.content: $\mathbb{P}(A)=1-\mathbb{P}(A^{c})$. Use when “at least one” appears and the complement (“none” / “all different”) is easier to count.
**The Birthday Paradox.** Assume 365 equally likely birthdays. Find the smallest $n$ such that $\mathbb{P}(\text{at least two people share a birthday})>\tfrac{1}{2}$.
blocks.solution:
$\mathbb{P}(\text{all distinct})=\prod_{k=0}^{n-1}(1-k/365)$. Approximation $\ln(1-x)\approx-x$: threshold at $n(n-1)>730\ln2\approx506$, so $\mathbf{n=23}$.

**Key takeaway:** Pairs grow as $\dbinom{n}{2}$, reaching $\sim365$ when $n\approx23$. General threshold: $n\approx1.18\sqrt{m}$ for pool size $m$.

blocks.title: Non-Adjacent Selection
blocks.content: Choose $k$ from $\{1,…,n\}$ with no two adjacent: $\dbinom{n-k+1}{k}$ ways. (Substitute $y_{i}=x_{i}-(i-1)$ to absorb gap constraints.)
**Probability of Choosing Three Non-Consecutive Integers.** From $\{1,…,10\}$, pick 3 distinct numbers uniformly at random. What is the probability no two are adjacent?
blocks.solution:
Total: $\dbinom{10}{3}=120$. Favourable: $\dbinom{8}{3}=56$. $\mathbb{P} = 56/120 = \mathbf{7/15}$.

**Key takeaway:** Memorise $\dbinom{n-k+1}{k}$ — it turns a constrained adjacency problem into an unconstrained one.

blocks.title: Inclusion-Exclusion Principle (IEP)
blocks.content: \[\mathbb{P}\!\Bigl(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_{i}\Bigr) = \sum_{i}\mathbb{P}(A_{i}) - \sum_{i<j}\mathbb{P}(A_{i}\cap A_{j}) + \sum_{i<j<k}\mathbb{P}(A_{i}\cap A_{j}\cap A_{k}) - \cdots\] Signal phrase: **“at least one”**.
**Probability of Consecutive Sixes in Three Dice Rolls.** Roll a fair six-sided die 3 times. What is the probability of getting at least one occurrence of the consecutive pattern $66$?
blocks.solution: Let $A=\{\text{rolls 1,2 both 6}\}$, $B=\{\text{rolls 2,3 both 6}\}$. \[\mathbb{P}(A\cup B)=\frac{1}{36}+\frac{1}{36}-\frac{1}{216}=\frac{11}{216}\approx5.1\%\] **Key takeaway:** Define $A_{i}=\{\text{pattern starts at }i\}$, apply IEP. Intersection $A_{i}\cap A_{i+1}$ is the longer overlapping pattern.

blocks.title: Circular Permutations
blocks.content: Fix one person: $(n-1)!$ total. Block of $k$ friends: $(n-k)!\cdot k!$ arrangements. \[\mathbb{P}(\text{$k$ friends consecutive}) = \frac{k!\,(n-k)!}{(n-1)!}\]
**Adjacent Friends Around a Round Table.** **Part 1.** 5 people, 2 friends. $\mathbb{P}(\text{friends adjacent})$? **Part 2.** Generalise to $n$ people, $k$ friends all consecutive.
blocks.solution:
**Part 1.** Fix one friend; 4 positions, 2 adjacent: $\mathbb{P}=\mathbf{1/2}$.

**Part 2.** $\mathbb{P} = \dfrac{k!\,(n-k)!}{(n-1)!}$. Verify $n=5, k=2$: $12/24=1/2$. ✓

**Key takeaway:** Always fix one person first. The block trick handles all consecutive-group problems.

blocks.content: **Probability Each Player Receives an Ace.** A 52-card deck is dealt to 4 players (13 cards each). What is the probability every player receives at least one ace?
blocks.solution:
**Step 1: Total number of equally likely deals.** A deal assigns 13 cards to each of 4 labeled players: \[|\Omega| = \binom{52}{13}\binom{39}{13}\binom{26}{13}\binom{13}{13}= \frac{52!}{(13!)^{4}}\]

**Step 2: Each player must receive exactly 1 ace.** There is no other way to give all 4 players at least one ace (only 4 aces exist). The number of ways to assign the 4 distinct aces to the 4 labeled players is: \[4!\]

**Step 3: Deal the remaining 48 non-ace cards.** Each player already has 1 ace and needs 12 more cards from the 48 non-aces: \[\binom{48}{12}\binom{36}{12}\binom{24}{12}\binom{12}{12}= \frac{48!}{(12!)^{4}}\]

**Step 4: Favorable deals.** \[|\text{favorable}| = 4!\cdot\frac{48!}{(12!)^{4}}\]

**Step 5: Compute the probability.** \[\mathbb{P}(\text{each player has an ace}) = \frac{4!\cdot\dfrac{48!}{(12!)^4}}{\dfrac{52!}{(13!)^4}}= 4!\cdot\frac{48!}{52!}\cdot\frac{(13!)^{4}}{(12!)^{4}}\]

**Step 6: Simplify.** \[\frac{48!}{52!}= \frac{1}{52\cdot51\cdot50\cdot49}, \qquad \left(\frac{13!}{12!}\right)^{4} = 13^{4}\] \[\mathbb{P}(\text{each player has an ace}) = \frac{4!\cdot 13^{4}}{52\cdot51\cdot50\cdot49}\] Using $52 = 4\cdot13$ to cancel with $4! = 24$: \[= \frac{24\cdot13^{4}}{4\cdot13\cdot51\cdot50\cdot49}= \frac{6\cdot13^{3}}{51\cdot50\cdot49}\]

\[\boxed{ \mathbb{P}(\text{each player has an ace}) = \frac{24\cdot 13^{4}}{52\cdot 51\cdot 50\cdot 49} = \frac{6\cdot 13^{3}}{51\cdot 50\cdot 49} = \frac{2197}{20825} \approx 10.5\% }\]

**Key takeaway:** The direct-counting approach — assign aces first, then deal non-aces — is cleaner than IEP here. Despite 4 aces for 4 players, the probability each receives one is only $\approx 1$ in $9.5$, because most deals concentrate two or more aces in the same hand.

blocks.title: Derangement Formula
blocks.content: $D_{n} = n!\sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{k}/k!\approx n!/e$. For $n\geq5$: $\mathbb{P}(\text{derangement})\approx1/e\approx36.8\%$.
**The Derangement Problem.** 5 firms, 5 cover letters placed randomly. Probability no letter goes to the correct firm?

blocks.title: Geometric Probability
blocks.content: $\mathbb{P}(A)=\text{measure of favourable region}/\text{total measure}$. Identify the right sample space, map the event geometrically, then compute. **Always draw the picture.**
**Probability All Random Points Lie in a Semicircle.** Pick $N$ points uniformly on a circle's circumference. What is the probability all $N$ lie within some semicircle?
blocks.solution: Let $A_{i}=\{\text{all other }N-1\text{ points in the semicircle clockwise from }i\}$. Events are mutually exclusive, $\mathbb{P}(A_{i})=(1/2)^{N-1}$. \[\boxed{\mathbb{P} = \frac{N}{2^{N-1}}}\] **Key takeaway:** Mutual exclusivity of $A_{1},…,A_{N}$ is the key step.

blocks.content: **Probability Two Random Chords Intersect.** Choose chords by picking four endpoints uniformly on a circle. What is the probability the chords intersect?
blocks.solution:
4 random points $\to$ 3 equally likely pairings. Chords intersect iff endpoints alternate; exactly 1 of 3 pairings does this. \[\boxed{\mathbb{P} = \frac{1}{3}}\]

**Warning:** Different chord-sampling methods give different answers (Bertrand's paradox). Always clarify the sampling method in an interview.

blocks.content: **Triangle Formation from Random Stick Breaks.** **(1)** Unit stick cut at two uniform points. $\mathbb{P}(\text{triangle})$? **(2)** First cut uniform; break the longer piece at a uniform point. $\mathbb{P}(\text{triangle})$?
blocks.solution:
**(1)** Event = each piece $<1/2$ = two triangles in $[0,1]^{2}$, total area $1/4$. $\boxed{\mathbb{P}_1=1/4}$.

**(2)** Condition on first cut $U\sim\mathrm{Unif}(0,\tfrac{1}{2})$, second $V\sim\mathrm{Unif}(U,1)$. Need $V>1/2$ and $V<U+1/2$: \[\mathbb{P}_{2} = 2\int_{0}^{1/2}\frac{U}{1-U}\,dU = 2\ln2-1\approx38.6\%\] **Key takeaway:** Breaking the longer piece raises the probability from 25\% to $\approx$38.6\%.

blocks.content:
**Average Chord Length of the Unit Circle.** What is the expected distance between two randomly chosen points on a unit circle?

*Hint: chord length $= 2|\sin(\Delta\theta/2)|$; integrate over $\Delta\theta\sim\mathrm{Unif*(0,\pi)$.}

**Balanced Assignment of Seniors Across Teams.** 16 people (4 seniors, 12 juniors) partitioned into 4 labeled groups of 4. $\mathbb{P}(\text{each group has exactly 1 senior})$?

*Hint: count ways to assign 4 seniors to 4 groups, one per group.*

**Topic 1 Technique Toolkit**
Uniform probabilityCount favourable / count total.
Complementary$\mathbb{P}(A)=1-\mathbb{P}(A^{c})$. Use for “at least one”.
SymmetryFair coin/dice: use symmetry before computing.
IEP$\mathbb{P}(\bigcup A_{i})=\sum\mathbb{P}(A_{i})-\sum\mathbb{P}(A_{i}\cap A_{j})+\cdots$
Non-adjacent$k$ from $n$, no two adjacent: $\dbinom{n-k+1}{k}$.
CircularFix one; block of $k$: $(n-k)!\cdot k!$.
Derangement$\mathbb{P}\approx1/e\approx36.8\%$ for $n\geq5$.
GeometricMap to region; compute area. Draw it.
Chord intersection4 pts $\to$ 3 pairings, 1 crosses: $\mathbb{P}=1/3$.
Semicircle containment$\mathbb{P}=N/2^{N-1}$.
